analogy: Think of "Periodic Classification of Elements" through the chapter's own opening images: Life Processes 5 CHAPTER How do we tell the difference between what is alive and what is not alive? If we see a dog running, or a cow chewing cud, or a man shouting loudly on the street, we know that these are living beings.... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Periodic Classification of Elements is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that life Processes 5 CHAPTER How do we tell the difference between what is alive and what is not alive?

The text explains that if we see a dog running, or a cow chewing cud, or a man shouting loudly on the street, we know that these are living beings.

The text explains that what if the dog or the cow or the man were asleep?

The text explains that we would still think that they were alive, but how did we know that?

The text explains that we see them breathing, and we know that they are alive.

The text explains that but what about plants that have leaves of colours other than green?

The text explains that they grow over time, so we know that they are alive, some will say.

The text explains that in other words, we tend to think of some sort of movement, either growth-related or not, as common evidence for being alive.

The text explains that but a plant that is not visibly growing is still alive, and some animals can breathe without visible movement.

The text explains that so using visible movement as the defining characteristic of life is not enough.

The text explains that movements over very small scales will be invisible to the naked eye – movements of molecules, for example.

The text explains that is this invisible molecular movement necessary for life?

The text explains that if we ask this question to professional biologists, they will say yes.

The text explains that in fact, viruses do not show any molecular movement in them (until they infect some cell), and that is partly why there is a controversy about whether they are truly alive or not.

The text explains that we have seen in earlier classes that living organisms are well-organised structures; they can have tissues, tissues have cells, cells have smaller components in them, and so on.

The text explains that because of the effects of the environment, this organised, ordered nature of living structures is very likely to keep breaking down over time.

The text explains that so there must be a process to transfer a source of energy from outside the body of the organism, which we call food, to the inside, a process we commonly call nutrition.

The text explains that since life on earth depends on carbon- based molecules, most of these food sources are also carbon-based.

The text explains that depending on the complexity of these carbon sources, different organisms can then use different kinds of nutritional processes.

The text explains that the outside sources of energy could be quite varied, since the environment is not under the control of the individual organism.

The text explains that these sources of energy, therefore, need to be broken down or built up in the body, and must be finally converted to a uniform source of energy that can be used for the various molecular movements needed for maintaining living structures, as well as to the kind of molecules the body needs to grow.

The text explains that for this, many organisms use oxygen sourced from outside the body.

The text explains that the process of acquiring oxygen from outside the body, and to use it in the process of break-down of food sources for cellular needs, is what we call respiration.

The text explains that in multi-cellular organisms, all the cells may not be in direct contact with the surrounding environment.

The text explains that when chemical reactions use the carbon source and the oxygen for energy generation, they create by-products that are not only useless for the cells of the body, but could even be harmful.

The text explains that this source of energy and materials is the food we eat.

The text explains that some organisms use simple food material obtained from inorganic sources in the form of carbon dioxide and water.

The text explains that thus, the heterotrophs survival depends directly or indirectly on autotrophs.

The text explains that for example, whether the food source is stationary (such as grass) or mobile (such as a deer), would allow for differences in how the food is accessed and what is the nutritive apparatus used by a cow and a lion.

The text explains that it begins when bacteria acting on sugars produce acids that softens or demineralises the enamel.

The text explains that the direction of diffusion depends upon the environmental conditions and the requirements of the plant.

The text explains that using tobacco directly or any product of tobacco in the form of cigar, cigarettes, bidis, hookah, gutkha, etc., is harmful.

The text explains that when the body size of animals is large, the diffusion pressure alone cannot take care of oxygen delivery to all parts of the body.

The text explains that this is useful in animals that have high energy needs, such as birds and mammals, which constantly use energy to maintain their body temperature.

The text explains that in animals that do not use energy for this purpose, the body temperature depends on the temperature in the environment.

The text explains that fishes, on the other hand, have only two chambers to their hearts, and the blood is pumped to the gills, is oxygenated there, and passes directly to the rest of the body.

The text explains that blood pressure is measured with an instrument called sphygmomanometer.

The text explains that high blood pressure is also called hypertension and is caused by the constriction of arterioles, which results in increased resistance to blood flow.

The text explains that blood pressure The force that blood exerts against the wall of a vessel is called blood pressure.

The text explains that this pressure is much greater in arteries than in veins.

The text explains that the pressure of blood inside the artery during ventricular systole (contraction) is called systolic pressure and pressure in artery during ventricular diastole (relaxation) is called diastolic pressure.

The text explains that the normal systolic pressure is about 120 mm of Hg and diastolic pressure is 80 mm of Hg.

The text explains that since the blood emerges from the heart under high pressure, the arteries have thick, elastic walls.

The text explains that they do not need thick walls because the blood is no longer under pressure, instead they have valves that ensure that the blood flows only in one direction.

The text explains that in addition, leakage would lead to a loss of pressure which would reduce the efficiency of the

The text explains that for plants, the soil is the nearest and richest source of raw materials like nitrogen, phosphorus and other minerals.

The text explains that one, the xylem moves water and minerals obtained from the soil.

The text explains that however, this pressure by itself is unlikely to be enough to move water over the heights that we commonly see in plants.

The text explains that thus, transpiration helps in the absorption and upward movement of water and minerals dissolved in it from roots to the leaves.

The text explains that the effect of root pressure in transport of water is more important at night.

The text explains that transport of food and other substances So far we have discussed the transport of water and minerals in plants.

The text explains that the translocation of food and other substances takes place in the sieve tubes with the help of adjacent companion cells both in upward and downward directions.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 5: Periodic Classification of Elements

prelims_traps: Do not treat 5 CHAPTER and Q U E S T I O N S as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Periodic Classification of Elements; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Periodic Classification of Elements through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 5 CHAPTER and Q U E S T I O N S to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
